Output

"0 /workspace/internal/terraform/node_resource_abstract_instance.go\n{\"content\":\"1: // Copyright (c) HashiCorp, Inc.\\n2: // SPDX-License-Identifier: BUSL-1.1\\n3: \\n4: package terraform\\n5: \\n6: import (\\n7: \\t\\\"fmt\\\"\\n8: \\t\\\"log\\\"\\n9: \\t\\\"strings\\\"\\n10: \\n11: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/hcl/v2\\\"\\n12: \\ttfaddr \\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform-registry-address\\\"\\n13: \\t\\\"github.com/zclconf/go-cty/cty\\\"\\n14: \\n15: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/addrs\\\"\\n16: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/checks\\\"\\n17: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs\\\"\\n18: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/configs/configschema\\\"\\n19: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/instances\\\"\\n20: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/lang/marks\\\"\\n21: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/moduletest/mocking\\\"\\n22: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans\\\"\\n23: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans/deferring\\\"\\n24: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/plans/objchange\\\"\\n25: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/providers\\\"\\n26: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/provisioners\\\"\\n27: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/states\\\"\\n28: \\t\\\"github.com/hashicorp/terraform/internal/tfdiags\\\"\\n29: )\\n30: \\n31: // NodeResourceInstanceBase represents a resource instance with no\\n32: // associated operations. It embeds NodeAbstractResource but additionally\\n33: // contains an instance key, used to identify one of potentially many\\n34: // instances that were created from a resource in configuration, e.g. using\\n35: // the \\\"count\\\" or \\\"for_each\\\" arguments.\\n36: type NodeResourceInstanceBase struct {\\n37: \\tNodeAbstractResource\\n38: \\tAddr addrs.AbsResourceInstance\\n39: \\n40: \\t// These are set via the AttachState method.\\n41: \\tinstanceState *states.ResourceInstance\\n42: \\n43: \\tDependencies []addrs.ConfigResource\\n44: \\n45: \\tpreDestroyRefresh bool\\n46: \\n47: \\t// During import we may generate configuration for a resource, which needs\\n48: \\t// to be stored in the final change.\\n49: \\tgeneratedConfigHCL string\\n50: \\n51: \\t// override is set by the graph itself, just before this node executes.\\n52: \\toverride *configs.Override\\n53: }\\n54: \\n55: // NewNodeResourceInstanceBase creates an abstract resource instance graph\\n56: // node for the given absolute resource instance address.\\n57: func NewNodeResourceInstanceBase(addr addrs.AbsResourceInstance) *NodeResourceInstanceBase {\\n58: \\t// Due to the fact that we embed NodeAbstractResource, the given address\\n59: \\t// actually ends up split between the resource address in the embedded\\n60: \\t// object and the InstanceKey field in our own struct. The\\n61: \\t// ResourceInstanceAddr method will stick these back together again on\\n62: \\t// request.\\n63: \\tr := NewNodeAbstractResource(addr.ContainingResource().Config())\\n64: \\treturn \\u0026NodeResourceInstanceBase{\\n65: \\t\\tNodeAbstractResource: *r,\\n66: \\t\\tAddr:                 addr,\\n67: \\t}\\n68: }\\n69: \\n70: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) Name() string {\\n71: \\treturn n.ResourceInstanceAddr().String()\\n72: }\\n73: \\n74: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) Path() addrs.ModuleInstance {\\n75: \\treturn n.Addr.Module\\n76: }\\n77: \\n78: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) HookResourceIdentity() HookResourceIdentity {\\n79: \\treturn HookResourceIdentity{\\n80: \\t\\tAddr:         n.Addr,\\n81: \\t\\tProviderAddr: n.ResolvedProvider.Provider,\\n82: \\t}\\n83: }\\n84: \\n85: // GraphNodeReferenceable\\n86: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) ReferenceableAddrs() []addrs.Referenceable {\\n87: \\taddr := n.ResourceInstanceAddr()\\n88: \\treturn []addrs.Referenceable{\\n89: \\t\\taddr.Resource,\\n90: \\n91: \\t\\t// A resource instance can also be referenced by the address of its\\n92: \\t\\t// containing resource, so that e.g. a reference to aws_instance.foo\\n93: \\t\\t// would match both aws_instance.foo[0] and aws_instance.foo[1].\\n94: \\t\\taddr.ContainingResource().Resource,\\n95: \\t}\\n96: }\\n97: \\n98: // GraphNodeReferencer\\n99: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) References() []*addrs.Reference {\\n100: \\t// If we have a configuration attached then we'll delegate to our\\n101: \\t// embedded abstract resource, which knows how to extract dependencies\\n102: \\t// from configuration. If there is no config, then the dependencies will\\n103: \\t// be connected during destroy from those stored in the state.\\n104: \\tif n.Config != nil {\\n105: \\t\\tif n.Schema == nil {\\n106: \\t\\t\\t// We'll produce a log message about this out here so that\\n107: \\t\\t\\t// we can include the full instance address, since the equivalent\\n108: \\t\\t\\t// message in NodeAbstractResource.References cannot see it.\\n109: \\t\\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"[WARN] no schema is attached to %s, so config references cannot be detected\\\", n.Name())\\n110: \\t\\t\\treturn nil\\n111: \\t\\t}\\n112: \\t\\treturn n.NodeAbstractResource.References()\\n113: \\t}\\n114: \\n115: \\t// If we have neither config nor state then we have no references.\\n116: \\treturn nil\\n117: }\\n118: \\n119: // StateDependencies returns the dependencies which will be saved in the state\\n120: // for managed resources, or the most current dependencies for data resources.\\n121: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) StateDependencies() []addrs.ConfigResource {\\n122: \\t// Managed resources prefer the stored dependencies, to avoid possible\\n123: \\t// conflicts in ordering when refactoring configuration.\\n124: \\tif s := n.instanceState; s != nil {\\n125: \\t\\tif s.Current != nil {\\n126: \\t\\t\\treturn s.Current.Dependencies\\n127: \\t\\t}\\n128: \\t}\\n129: \\n130: \\t// If there are no stored dependencies, this is either a newly created\\n131: \\t// managed resource, or a data source, and we can use the most recently\\n132: \\t// calculated dependencies.\\n133: \\treturn n.Dependencies\\n134: }\\n135: \\n136: // GraphNodeResourceInstance\\n137: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) ResourceInstanceAddr() addrs.AbsResourceInstance {\\n138: \\treturn n.Addr\\n139: }\\n140: \\n141: // GraphNodeAttachResourceState\\n142: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) AttachResourceState(s *states.Resource) {\\n143: \\tif s == nil {\\n144: \\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"[WARN] attaching nil state to %s\\\", n.Addr)\\n145: \\t\\treturn\\n146: \\t}\\n147: \\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] NodeResourceInstanceBase.AttachResourceState for %s\\\", n.Addr)\\n148: \\tn.instanceState = s.Instance(n.Addr.Resource.Key)\\n149: \\tn.storedProviderConfig = s.ProviderConfig\\n150: }\\n151: \\n152: // GraphNodeOverridable\\n153: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) ConfigProvider() addrs.AbsProviderConfig {\\n154: \\treturn n.ResolvedProvider\\n155: }\\n156: \\n157: // GraphNodeOverridable\\n158: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) SetOverride(override *configs.Override) {\\n159: \\tn.override = override\\n160: }\\n161: \\n162: // readDiff returns the planned change for a particular resource instance\\n163: // object.\\n164: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) readDiff(ctx EvalContext, providerSchema providers.ProviderSchema) (*plans.ResourceInstanceChange, error) {\\n165: \\tchanges := ctx.Changes()\\n166: \\taddr := n.ResourceInstanceAddr()\\n167: \\n168: \\tschema, _ := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(addr.Resource.Resource)\\n169: \\tif schema == nil {\\n170: \\t\\t// Should be caught during validation, so we don't bother with a pretty error here\\n171: \\t\\treturn nil, fmt.Errorf(\\\"provider does not support resource type %q\\\", addr.Resource.Resource.Type)\\n172: \\t}\\n173: \\n174: \\tchange := changes.GetResourceInstanceChange(addr, addrs.NotDeposed)\\n175: \\n176: \\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] readDiff: Read %s change from plan for %s\\\", change.Action, n.Addr)\\n177: \\n178: \\treturn change, nil\\n179: }\\n180: \\n181: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) checkPreventDestroy(change *plans.ResourceInstanceChange) tfdiags.Diagnostics {\\n182: \\tif change == nil || n.Config == nil || n.Config.Managed == nil {\\n183: \\t\\treturn nil\\n184: \\t}\\n185: \\n186: \\tpreventDestroy := n.Config.Managed.PreventDestroy\\n187: \\n188: \\tif (change.Action == plans.Delete || change.Action.IsReplace()) \\u0026\\u0026 preventDestroy {\\n189: \\t\\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\\n190: \\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(\\u0026hcl.Diagnostic{\\n191: \\t\\t\\tSeverity: hcl.DiagError,\\n192: \\t\\t\\tSummary:  \\\"Instance cannot be destroyed\\\",\\n193: \\t\\t\\tDetail: fmt.Sprintf(\\n194: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"Resource %s has lifecycle.prevent_destroy set, but the plan calls for this resource to be destroyed. To avoid this error and continue with the plan, either disable lifecycle.prevent_destroy or reduce the scope of the plan using the -target option.\\\",\\n195: \\t\\t\\t\\tn.Addr.String(),\\n196: \\t\\t\\t),\\n197: \\t\\t\\tSubject: \\u0026n.Config.DeclRange,\\n198: \\t\\t})\\n199: \\t\\treturn diags\\n200: \\t}\\n201: \\n202: \\treturn nil\\n203: }\\n204: \\n205: // preApplyHook calls the pre-Apply hook\\n206: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) preApplyHook(ctx EvalContext, change *plans.ResourceInstanceChange) tfdiags.Diagnostics {\\n207: \\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\\n208: \\n209: \\tif change == nil {\\n210: \\t\\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\\\"preApplyHook for %s called with nil Change\\\", n.Addr))\\n211: \\t}\\n212: \\n213: \\t// Only managed resources have user-visible apply actions.\\n214: \\tif n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Mode == addrs.ManagedResourceMode {\\n215: \\t\\tpriorState := change.Before\\n216: \\t\\tplannedNewState := change.After\\n217: \\n218: \\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\\n219: \\t\\t\\treturn h.PreApply(n.HookResourceIdentity(), change.DeposedKey, change.Action, priorState, plannedNewState)\\n220: \\t\\t}))\\n221: \\t\\tif diags.HasErrors() {\\n222: \\t\\t\\treturn diags\\n223: \\t\\t}\\n224: \\t}\\n225: \\n226: \\treturn nil\\n227: }\\n228: \\n229: // postApplyHook calls the post-Apply hook\\n230: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) postApplyHook(ctx EvalContext, state *states.ResourceInstanceObject, err error) tfdiags.Diagnostics {\\n231: \\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\\n232: \\n233: \\t// Only managed resources have user-visible apply actions.\\n234: \\tif n.Addr.Resource.Resource.Mode == addrs.ManagedResourceMode {\\n235: \\t\\tvar newState cty.Value\\n236: \\t\\tif state != nil {\\n237: \\t\\t\\tnewState = state.Value\\n238: \\t\\t} else {\\n239: \\t\\t\\tnewState = cty.NullVal(cty.DynamicPseudoType)\\n240: \\t\\t}\\n241: \\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(ctx.Hook(func(h Hook) (HookAction, error) {\\n242: \\t\\t\\treturn h.PostApply(n.HookResourceIdentity(), addrs.NotDeposed, newState, err)\\n243: \\t\\t}))\\n244: \\t}\\n245: \\n246: \\treturn diags\\n247: }\\n248: \\n249: type phaseState int\\n250: \\n251: const (\\n252: \\tworkingState phaseState = iota\\n253: \\trefreshState\\n254: \\tprevRunState\\n255: )\\n256: \\n257: //go:generate go run golang.org/x/tools/cmd/stringer -type phaseState\\n258: \\n259: // writeResourceInstanceState saves the given object as the current object for\\n260: // the selected resource instance.\\n261: //\\n262: // dependencies is a parameter, instead of those directly attacted to the\\n263: // NodeResourceInstanceBase, because we don't write dependencies for\\n264: // datasources.\\n265: //\\n266: // targetState determines which context state we're writing to during plan. The\\n267: // default is the global working state.\\n268: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) writeResourceInstanceState(ctx EvalContext, obj *states.ResourceInstanceObject, targetState phaseState) error {\\n269: \\treturn n.writeResourceInstanceStateImpl(ctx, states.NotDeposed, obj, targetState)\\n270: }\\n271: \\n272: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) writeResourceInstanceStateDeposed(ctx EvalContext, deposedKey states.DeposedKey, obj *states.ResourceInstanceObject, targetState phaseState) error {\\n273: \\tif deposedKey == states.NotDeposed {\\n274: \\t\\t// Bail out to avoid silently doing something other than what the\\n275: \\t\\t// caller seems to have intended.\\n276: \\t\\tpanic(\\\"trying to write current state object using writeResourceInstanceStateDeposed\\\")\\n277: \\t}\\n278: \\treturn n.writeResourceInstanceStateImpl(ctx, deposedKey, obj, targetState)\\n279: }\\n280: \\n281: // (this is the private common body of both writeResourceInstanceState and\\n282: // writeResourceInstanceStateDeposed. Don't call it directly; instead, use\\n283: // one of the two wrappers to be explicit about which of the instance's\\n284: // objects you are intending to write.\\n285: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) writeResourceInstanceStateImpl(ctx EvalContext, deposedKey states.DeposedKey, obj *states.ResourceInstanceObject, targetState phaseState) error {\\n286: \\tabsAddr := n.Addr\\n287: \\t_, providerSchema, err := getProvider(ctx, n.ResolvedProvider)\\n288: \\tif err != nil {\\n289: \\t\\treturn err\\n290: \\t}\\n291: \\tlogFuncName := \\\"NodeResourceInstanceBase.writeResourceInstanceState\\\"\\n292: \\tif deposedKey == states.NotDeposed {\\n293: \\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] %s to %s for %s\\\", logFuncName, targetState, absAddr)\\n294: \\t} else {\\n295: \\t\\tlogFuncName = \\\"NodeResourceInstanceBase.writeResourceInstanceStateDeposed\\\"\\n296: \\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] %s to %s for %s (deposed key %s)\\\", logFuncName, targetState, absAddr, deposedKey)\\n297: \\t}\\n298: \\n299: \\tvar state *states.SyncState\\n300: \\tswitch targetState {\\n301: \\tcase workingState:\\n302: \\t\\tstate = ctx.State()\\n303: \\tcase refreshState:\\n304: \\t\\tstate = ctx.RefreshState()\\n305: \\tcase prevRunState:\\n306: \\t\\tstate = ctx.PrevRunState()\\n307: \\tdefault:\\n308: \\t\\tpanic(fmt.Sprintf(\\\"unsupported phaseState value %#v\\\", targetState))\\n309: \\t}\\n310: \\tif state == nil {\\n311: \\t\\t// Should not happen, because we shouldn't ever try to write to\\n312: \\t\\t// a state that isn't applicable to the current operation.\\n313: \\t\\t// (We can also get in here for unit tests which are using\\n314: \\t\\t// EvalContextMock but not populating PrevRunStateState with\\n315: \\t\\t// a suitable state object.)\\n316: \\t\\treturn fmt.Errorf(\\\"state of type %s is not applicable to the current operation; this is a bug in Terraform\\\", targetState)\\n317: \\t}\\n318: \\n319: \\t// In spite of the name, this function also handles the non-deposed case\\n320: \\t// via the writeResourceInstanceState wrapper, by setting deposedKey to\\n321: \\t// the NotDeposed value (the zero value of DeposedKey).\\n322: \\tvar write func(src *states.ResourceInstanceObjectSrc)\\n323: \\tif deposedKey == states.NotDeposed {\\n324: \\t\\twrite = func(src *states.ResourceInstanceObjectSrc) {\\n325: \\t\\t\\tstate.SetResourceInstanceCurrent(absAddr, src, n.ResolvedProvider)\\n326: \\t\\t}\\n327: \\t} else {\\n328: \\t\\twrite = func(src *states.ResourceInstanceObjectSrc) {\\n329: \\t\\t\\tstate.SetResourceInstanceDeposed(absAddr, deposedKey, src, n.ResolvedProvider)\\n330: \\t\\t}\\n331: \\t}\\n332: \\n333: \\tif obj == nil || obj.Value.IsNull() {\\n334: \\t\\t// No need to encode anything: we'll just write it directly.\\n335: \\t\\twrite(nil)\\n336: \\t\\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] %s: removing state object for %s\\\", logFuncName, absAddr)\\n337: \\t\\treturn nil\\n338: \\t}\\n339: \\n340: \\tlog.Printf(\\\"[TRACE] %s: writing state object for %s\\\", logFuncName, absAddr)\\n341: \\n342: \\tschema, currentVersion := providerSchema.SchemaForResourceAddr(absAddr.ContainingResource().Resource)\\n343: \\tif schema == nil {\\n344: \\t\\t// I\n[... Observation truncated due to length ...]\n= diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\\n2580: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\ttfdiags.Error,\\n2581: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"Provider returned invalid result object after apply\\\",\\n2582: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\n2583: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"After the apply operation, the provider still indicated an unknown value for %s%s. All values must be known after apply, so this is always a bug in the provider and should be reported in the provider's own repository. Terraform will still save the other known object values in the state.\\\",\\n2584: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tn.Addr, pathStr,\\n2585: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n2586: \\t\\t\\t\\t))\\n2587: \\t\\t\\t}\\n2588: \\t\\t\\treturn true, nil\\n2589: \\t\\t})\\n2590: \\n2591: \\t\\t// NOTE: This operation can potentially be lossy if there are multiple\\n2592: \\t\\t// elements in a set that differ only by unknown values: after\\n2593: \\t\\t// replacing with null these will be merged together into a single set\\n2594: \\t\\t// element. Since we can only get here in the presence of a provider\\n2595: \\t\\t// bug, we accept this because storing a result here is always a\\n2596: \\t\\t// best-effort sort of thing.\\n2597: \\t\\tnewVal = cty.UnknownAsNull(newVal)\\n2598: \\t}\\n2599: \\n2600: \\t// If we have paths to mark, mark those on this new value we need to\\n2601: \\t// re-check the value against the schema, because nested computed values\\n2602: \\t// won't be included in afterPaths, which are only what was read from the\\n2603: \\t// After plan value.\\n2604: \\tnewVal = newVal.MarkWithPaths(afterPaths)\\n2605: \\tif sensitivePaths := schema.SensitivePaths(newVal, nil); len(sensitivePaths) != 0 {\\n2606: \\t\\tnewVal = marks.MarkPaths(newVal, marks.Sensitive, sensitivePaths)\\n2607: \\t}\\n2608: \\n2609: \\tif change.Action != plans.Delete \\u0026\\u0026 !diags.HasErrors() {\\n2610: \\t\\t// Only values that were marked as unknown in the planned value are allowed\\n2611: \\t\\t// to change during the apply operation. (We do this after the unknown-ness\\n2612: \\t\\t// check above so that we also catch anything that became unknown after\\n2613: \\t\\t// being known during plan.)\\n2614: \\t\\t//\\n2615: \\t\\t// If we are returning other errors anyway then we'll give this\\n2616: \\t\\t// a pass since the other errors are usually the explanation for\\n2617: \\t\\t// this one and so it's more helpful to let the user focus on the\\n2618: \\t\\t// root cause rather than distract with this extra problem.\\n2619: \\t\\tif errs := objchange.AssertObjectCompatible(schema, change.After, newVal); len(errs) \\u003e 0 {\\n2620: \\t\\t\\tif resp.LegacyTypeSystem {\\n2621: \\t\\t\\t\\t// The shimming of the old type system in the legacy SDK is not precise\\n2622: \\t\\t\\t\\t// enough to pass this consistency check, so we'll give it a pass here,\\n2623: \\t\\t\\t\\t// but we will generate a warning about it so that we are more likely\\n2624: \\t\\t\\t\\t// to notice in the logs if an inconsistency beyond the type system\\n2625: \\t\\t\\t\\t// leads to a downstream provider failure.\\n2626: \\t\\t\\t\\tvar buf strings.Builder\\n2627: \\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Fprintf(\\u0026buf, \\\"[WARN] Provider %q produced an unexpected new value for %s, but we are tolerating it because it is using the legacy plugin SDK.\\\\n    The following problems may be the cause of any confusing errors from downstream operations:\\\", n.ResolvedProvider.String(), n.Addr)\\n2628: \\t\\t\\t\\tfor _, err := range errs {\\n2629: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Fprintf(\\u0026buf, \\\"\\\\n      - %s\\\", tfdiags.FormatError(err))\\n2630: \\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n2631: \\t\\t\\t\\tlog.Print(buf.String())\\n2632: \\n2633: \\t\\t\\t\\t// The sort of inconsistency we won't catch here is if a known value\\n2634: \\t\\t\\t\\t// in the plan is changed during apply. That can cause downstream\\n2635: \\t\\t\\t\\t// problems because a dependent resource would make its own plan based\\n2636: \\t\\t\\t\\t// on the planned value, and thus get a different result during the\\n2637: \\t\\t\\t\\t// apply phase. This will usually lead to a \\\"Provider produced invalid plan\\\"\\n2638: \\t\\t\\t\\t// error that incorrectly blames the downstream resource for the change.\\n2639: \\n2640: \\t\\t\\t} else {\\n2641: \\t\\t\\t\\tfor _, err := range errs {\\n2642: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\\n2643: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\ttfdiags.Error,\\n2644: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"Provider produced inconsistent result after apply\\\",\\n2645: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\n2646: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"When applying changes to %s, provider %q produced an unexpected new value: %s.\\\\n\\\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\\\",\\n2647: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tn.Addr, n.ResolvedProvider.String(), tfdiags.FormatError(err),\\n2648: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n2649: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t))\\n2650: \\t\\t\\t\\t}\\n2651: \\t\\t\\t}\\n2652: \\t\\t}\\n2653: \\t}\\n2654: \\n2655: \\t// If a provider returns a null or non-null object at the wrong time then\\n2656: \\t// we still want to save that but it often causes some confusing behaviors\\n2657: \\t// where it seems like Terraform is failing to take any action at all,\\n2658: \\t// so we'll generate some errors to draw attention to it.\\n2659: \\tif !diags.HasErrors() {\\n2660: \\t\\tif change.Action == plans.Delete \\u0026\\u0026 !newVal.IsNull() {\\n2661: \\t\\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\\n2662: \\t\\t\\t\\ttfdiags.Error,\\n2663: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"Provider returned invalid result object after apply\\\",\\n2664: \\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\n2665: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"After applying a %s plan, the provider returned a non-null object for %s. Destroying should always produce a null value, so this is always a bug in the provider and should be reported in the provider's own repository. Terraform will still save this errant object in the state for debugging and recovery.\\\",\\n2666: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tchange.Action, n.Addr,\\n2667: \\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n2668: \\t\\t\\t))\\n2669: \\t\\t}\\n2670: \\t\\tif change.Action != plans.Delete \\u0026\\u0026 newVal.IsNull() {\\n2671: \\t\\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\\n2672: \\t\\t\\t\\ttfdiags.Error,\\n2673: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"Provider returned invalid result object after apply\\\",\\n2674: \\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\n2675: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"After applying a %s plan, the provider returned a null object for %s. Only destroying should always produce a null value, so this is always a bug in the provider and should be reported in the provider's own repository.\\\",\\n2676: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tchange.Action, n.Addr,\\n2677: \\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n2678: \\t\\t\\t))\\n2679: \\t\\t}\\n2680: \\t}\\n2681: \\n2682: \\tswitch {\\n2683: \\tcase diags.HasErrors() \\u0026\\u0026 newVal.IsNull():\\n2684: \\t\\t// Sometimes providers return a null value when an operation fails for\\n2685: \\t\\t// some reason, but we'd rather keep the prior state so that the error\\n2686: \\t\\t// can be corrected on a subsequent run. We must only do this for null\\n2687: \\t\\t// new value though, or else we may discard partial updates the\\n2688: \\t\\t// provider was able to complete. Otherwise, we'll continue using the\\n2689: \\t\\t// prior state as the new value, making this effectively a no-op.  If\\n2690: \\t\\t// the item really _has_ been deleted then our next refresh will detect\\n2691: \\t\\t// that and fix it up.\\n2692: \\t\\treturn state.DeepCopy(), diags\\n2693: \\n2694: \\tcase diags.HasErrors() \\u0026\\u0026 !newVal.IsNull():\\n2695: \\t\\t// if we have an error, make sure we restore the object status in the new state\\n2696: \\t\\tnewState := \\u0026states.ResourceInstanceObject{\\n2697: \\t\\t\\tStatus:              state.Status,\\n2698: \\t\\t\\tValue:               newVal,\\n2699: \\t\\t\\tPrivate:             resp.Private,\\n2700: \\t\\t\\tCreateBeforeDestroy: createBeforeDestroy,\\n2701: \\t\\t}\\n2702: \\n2703: \\t\\t// if the resource was being deleted, the dependencies are not going to\\n2704: \\t\\t// be recalculated and we need to restore those as well.\\n2705: \\t\\tif change.Action == plans.Delete {\\n2706: \\t\\t\\tnewState.Dependencies = state.Dependencies\\n2707: \\t\\t}\\n2708: \\n2709: \\t\\treturn newState, diags\\n2710: \\n2711: \\tcase !newVal.IsNull():\\n2712: \\t\\t// Non error case with a new state\\n2713: \\t\\tnewState := \\u0026states.ResourceInstanceObject{\\n2714: \\t\\t\\tStatus:              states.ObjectReady,\\n2715: \\t\\t\\tValue:               newVal,\\n2716: \\t\\t\\tPrivate:             resp.Private,\\n2717: \\t\\t\\tCreateBeforeDestroy: createBeforeDestroy,\\n2718: \\t\\t}\\n2719: \\t\\treturn newState, diags\\n2720: \\n2721: \\tdefault:\\n2722: \\t\\t// Non error case, were the object was deleted\\n2723: \\t\\treturn nil, diags\\n2724: \\t}\\n2725: }\\n2726: \\n2727: func (n *NodeResourceInstanceBase) prevRunAddr(ctx EvalContext) addrs.AbsResourceInstance {\\n2728: \\treturn resourceInstancePrevRunAddr(ctx, n.Addr)\\n2729: }\\n2730: \\n2731: func resourceInstancePrevRunAddr(ctx EvalContext, currentAddr addrs.AbsResourceInstance) addrs.AbsResourceInstance {\\n2732: \\ttable := ctx.MoveResults()\\n2733: \\treturn table.OldAddr(currentAddr)\\n2734: }\\n2735: \\n2736: func getAction(addr addrs.AbsResourceInstance, priorVal, plannedNewVal cty.Value, createBeforeDestroy bool, forceReplace []addrs.AbsResourceInstance, reqRep cty.PathSet) (action plans.Action, actionReason plans.ResourceInstanceChangeActionReason) {\\n2737: \\t// The user might also ask us to force replacing a particular resource\\n2738: \\t// instance, regardless of whether the provider thinks it needs replacing.\\n2739: \\t// For example, users typically do this if they learn a particular object\\n2740: \\t// has become degraded in an immutable infrastructure scenario and so\\n2741: \\t// replacing it with a new object is a viable repair path.\\n2742: \\tmatchedForceReplace := false\\n2743: \\tfor _, candidateAddr := range forceReplace {\\n2744: \\t\\tif candidateAddr.Equal(addr) {\\n2745: \\t\\t\\tmatchedForceReplace = true\\n2746: \\t\\t\\tbreak\\n2747: \\t\\t}\\n2748: \\n2749: \\t\\t// For \\\"force replace\\\" purposes we require an exact resource instance\\n2750: \\t\\t// address to match. If a user forgets to include the instance key\\n2751: \\t\\t// for a multi-instance resource then it won't match here, but we\\n2752: \\t\\t// have an earlier check in NodePlannableResource.Execute that should\\n2753: \\t\\t// prevent us from getting here in that case.\\n2754: \\t}\\n2755: \\n2756: \\t// Unmark for this test for value equality.\\n2757: \\teqV := plannedNewVal.Equals(priorVal)\\n2758: \\teq := eqV.IsKnown() \\u0026\\u0026 eqV.True()\\n2759: \\n2760: \\tswitch {\\n2761: \\tcase priorVal.IsNull():\\n2762: \\t\\taction = plans.Create\\n2763: \\tcase eq \\u0026\\u0026 !matchedForceReplace:\\n2764: \\t\\taction = plans.NoOp\\n2765: \\tcase matchedForceReplace || !reqRep.Empty():\\n2766: \\t\\t// If the user \\\"forced replace\\\" of this instance of if there are any\\n2767: \\t\\t// \\\"requires replace\\\" paths left _after our filtering above_ then this\\n2768: \\t\\t// is a replace action.\\n2769: \\t\\tif createBeforeDestroy {\\n2770: \\t\\t\\taction = plans.CreateThenDelete\\n2771: \\t\\t} else {\\n2772: \\t\\t\\taction = plans.DeleteThenCreate\\n2773: \\t\\t}\\n2774: \\t\\tswitch {\\n2775: \\t\\tcase matchedForceReplace:\\n2776: \\t\\t\\tactionReason = plans.ResourceInstanceReplaceByRequest\\n2777: \\t\\tcase !reqRep.Empty():\\n2778: \\t\\t\\tactionReason = plans.ResourceInstanceReplaceBecauseCannotUpdate\\n2779: \\t\\t}\\n2780: \\tdefault:\\n2781: \\t\\taction = plans.Update\\n2782: \\t\\t// \\\"Delete\\\" is never chosen here, because deletion plans are always\\n2783: \\t\\t// created more directly elsewhere, such as in \\\"orphan\\\" handling.\\n2784: \\t}\\n2785: \\n2786: \\treturn\\n2787: }\\n2788: \\n2789: // getRequiredReplaces returns a list of paths to attributes whose changes mean\\n2790: // that we must replace rather than update an existing remote object.\\n2791: //\\n2792: // The provider produces a list of paths to attributes whose changes mean\\n2793: // that we must replace rather than update an existing remote object.\\n2794: // However, we only need to do that if the identified attributes _have_\\n2795: // actually changed -- particularly after we may have undone some of the\\n2796: // changes in processIgnoreChanges -- so now we'll filter that list to\\n2797: // include only where changes are detected.\\n2798: //\\n2799: // Both the priorVal and plannedNewVal should be unmarked before calling this\\n2800: // function. This function exposes nothing about the priorVal or plannedVal\\n2801: // except for the paths that require replacement which can be deduced from the\\n2802: // type with or without marks.\\n2803: func getRequiredReplaces(priorVal, plannedNewVal cty.Value, requiredReplaces []cty.Path, providerAddr tfaddr.Provider, addr addrs.AbsResourceInstance) (cty.PathSet, tfdiags.Diagnostics) {\\n2804: \\tvar diags tfdiags.Diagnostics\\n2805: \\n2806: \\treqRep := cty.NewPathSet()\\n2807: \\tif len(requiredReplaces) \\u003e 0 {\\n2808: \\t\\tfor _, path := range requiredReplaces {\\n2809: \\t\\t\\tif priorVal.IsNull() {\\n2810: \\t\\t\\t\\t// If prior is null then we don't expect any RequiresReplace at all,\\n2811: \\t\\t\\t\\t// because this is a Create action.\\n2812: \\t\\t\\t\\tcontinue\\n2813: \\t\\t\\t}\\n2814: \\n2815: \\t\\t\\tpriorChangedVal, priorPathDiags := hcl.ApplyPath(priorVal, path, nil)\\n2816: \\t\\t\\tplannedChangedVal, plannedPathDiags := hcl.ApplyPath(plannedNewVal, path, nil)\\n2817: \\t\\t\\tif plannedPathDiags.HasErrors() \\u0026\\u0026 priorPathDiags.HasErrors() {\\n2818: \\t\\t\\t\\t// This means the path was invalid in both the prior and new\\n2819: \\t\\t\\t\\t// values, which is an error with the provider itself.\\n2820: \\t\\t\\t\\tdiags = diags.Append(tfdiags.Sourceless(\\n2821: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\ttfdiags.Error,\\n2822: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"Provider produced invalid plan\\\",\\n2823: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\tfmt.Sprintf(\\n2824: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\\"Provider %q has indicated \\\\\\\"requires replacement\\\\\\\" on %s for a non-existent attribute path %#v.\\\\n\\\\nThis is a bug in the provider, which should be reported in the provider's own issue tracker.\\\",\\n2825: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t\\tproviderAddr, addr, path,\\n2826: \\t\\t\\t\\t\\t),\\n2827: \\t\\t\\t\\t))\\n2828: \\t\\t\\t\\tcontinue\\n2829: \\t\\t\\t}\\n2830: \\n2831: \\t\\t\\t// Make sure we have valid Values for both values.\\n2832: \\t\\t\\t// Note: if the opposing value was of the type\\n2833: \\t\\t\\t// cty.DynamicPseudoType, the type assigned here may not exactly\\n2834: \\t\\t\\t// match the schema. This is fine here, since we're only going to\\n2835: \\t\\t\\t// check for equality, but if the NullVal is to be used, we need to\\n2836: \\t\\t\\t// check the schema for th true type.\\n2837: \\t\\t\\tswitch {\\n2838: \\t\\t\\tcase priorChangedVal == cty.NilVal \\u0026\\u0026 plannedChangedVal == cty.NilVal:\\n2839: \\t\\t\\t\\t// this should never happen without ApplyPath errors above\\n2840: \\t\\t\\t\\tpanic(\\\"requires replace path returned 2 nil values\\\")\\n2841: \\t\\t\\tcase priorChangedVal == cty.NilVal:\\n2842: \\t\\t\\t\\tpriorChangedVal = cty.NullVal(plannedChangedVal.Type())\\n2843: \\t\\t\\tcase plannedChangedVal == cty.NilVal:\\n2844: \\t\\t\\t\\tplannedChangedVal = cty.NullVal(priorChangedVal.Type())\\n2845: \\t\\t\\t}\\n2846: \\n2847: \\t\\t\\teqV := plannedChangedVal.Equals(priorChangedVal)\\n2848: \\t\\t\\tif !eqV.IsKnown() || eqV.False() {\\n2849: \\t\\t\\t\\treqRep.Add(path)\\n2850: \\t\\t\\t}\\n2851: \\t\\t}\\n2852: \\t\\tif diags.HasErrors() {\\n2853: \\t\\t\\treturn reqRep, diags\\n2854: \\t\\t}\\n2855: \\t}\\n2856: \\n2857: \\treturn reqRep, diags\\n2858: }\\n2859: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/terraform--v1.10.3@9802216fb24076bc614d9fa7961a9d85f6d7ec48/-/blob/internal/terraform/node_resource_abstract_instance.go\"}"

Input

{
  "thought": "The issue is that the MCP tool returns the ENTIRE file content as a JSON string with escaped Unicode chars (like `\\n`, `\\t`, `\\u0026`, etc.). The Python regex to strip line numbers `re.sub(r'^\\d+: ', '', raw, flags=re.MULTILINE)` doesn't work because the content is a JSON-encoded string, not actual lines.\n\nThe file content is returned as: `{\"content\":\"1: // ...\\n2: // ...\\n...2859: \"}` \n\nThe issue is that the MCP tool returns the JSON-encoded content and my Python script is getting the raw JSON string (including the unicode escapes) rather than the decoded content.\n\nLet me fix the Python script to properly parse the JSON content:\n- The response contains a `content` field with the text \n- The text has line numbers like \"1: // Copyright ...\"\n- But it's JSON encoded, so `\\n` is the actual newline in the JSON string\n\nWait, looking at the output more carefully:\n- Line 1: `0 /workspace/internal/terraform/node_resource_abstract_instance.go` - the file has 0 lines!\n- Then I see the actual content that should have been written\n\nIt seems the file was written but with 0 lines. That means the file content was written as an empty string, or the write failed.\n\nActually wait, looking at the output again - the file has \"0 lines\" but then we see the content that SHOULD have been written. This means the Python script output the content to STDOUT (through the `print` statement) but something went wrong with writing to the file.\n\nNo wait - looking at the output more carefully:\n- `0 /workspace/internal/terraform/node_resource_abstract_instance.go` means the file has 0 lines\n- Then there's a big JSON object printed - that's from the head command showing the file content\n\nActually `wc -l` reports 0 lines for empty files. And the `head -5` output shows what looks like the full JSON content of the MCP response written to the file... but the file is 0 lines?\n\nWait - `head -5` truncates to 5 lines from the file. And the output I see IS the JSON content. But `wc -l` says 0. That means the JSON content has no newlines (it's all on one line). The `wc -l` command counts newlines (it doesn't count the last line if it doesn't end with a newline).\n\nSo the file WAS written, but it contains the RAW JSON response from the MCP API, not the Go source code. The Python script failed to properly extract the content.\n\nThe issue is in my Python script: `data = json.loads(line[5:])` - this parses one JSON line. But the MCP response's text content field is itself a JSON-encoded string containing the file content. The content field has newlines encoded as `\\n` which JSON decodes to actual newlines.\n\nLet me look at the data structure:\n```\ndata['result']['content'][0]['text'] = '{\"content\":\"1: // ...\\n...\",\"url\":\"...\"}'\n```\n\nSo `item['text']` contains a JSON string that itself contains `content` and `url` fields. This is a nested JSON encoding.\n\nI need to parse the inner JSON too:\n```python\ninner = json.loads(item['text'])\nraw = inner['content']\n```\n\nThen strip line numbers from `raw`.\n\nLet me fix the script.\n"
}

{
  "command": "cat > /tmp/fetch_and_transform2.py << 'EOF'\nimport subprocess, json, re, sys\n\nresult = subprocess.run(\n    ['curl', '-s', '-X', 'POST', 'http://127.0.0.1:18973/mcp',\n     '-H', 'Content-Type: application/json',\n     '-d', json.dumps({\n         \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\", \"id\": 3, \"method\": \"tools/call\",\n         \"params\": {\"name\": \"read_file\", \"arguments\": {\n             \"repo\": \"github.com/sg-evals/terraform--v1.10.3\",\n             \"path\": \"internal/terraform/node_resource_abstract_instance.go\"\n         }}\n     })],\n    capture_output=True, text=True\n)\n\nfor line in result.stdout.split('\\n'):\n    if line.startswith('data:'):\n        data = json.loads(line[5:])\n        if 'result' in data:\n            for item in data['result']['content']:\n                if item['type'] == 'text':\n                    # The text is itself a JSON-encoded object with 'content' field\n                    inner = json.loads(item['text'])\n                    raw = inner['content']\n                    # Strip line numbers (format: \"N: content\\n\")\n                    go_lines = re.sub(r'^\\d+: ', '', raw, flags=re.MULTILINE)\n                    # Apply substitutions\n                    go_lines = go_lines.replace('NodeAbstractResouceInstance', 'NodeResourceInstanceBase')\n                    go_lines = go_lines.replace('NewNodeAbstractResourceInstance', 'NewNodeResourceInstanceBase')\n                    go_lines = go_lines.replace('NodeAbstractResourceInstance', 'NodeResourceInstanceBase')\n                    with open('/workspace/internal/terraform/node_resource_abstract_instance.go', 'w') as f:\n                        f.write(go_lines)\n                    lines = go_lines.splitlines()\n                    print(f\"Written {len(lines)} lines\")\n                    # Verify substitutions were applied\n                    occurrences = go_lines.count('NodeAbstractResourceInstance')\n                    print(f\"Remaining 'NodeAbstractResourceInstance' occurrences: {occurrences}\")\n                    occurrences_new = go_lines.count('NodeResourceInstanceBase')\n                    print(f\"'NodeResourceInstanceBase' occurrences: {occurrences_new}\")\n                    sys.exit(0)\n\nprint(\"ERROR: Could not fetch file\", file=sys.stderr)\nsys.exit(1)\nEOF\npython3 /tmp/fetch_and_transform2.py",
  "security_risk": "MEDIUM"
}

68. execute_bash @ 2026-03-11T18:42:48.670313

Input

{
  "command": "cat > /tmp/fetch_file.py << 'EOF'\n#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"Fetch a file from Sourcegraph via local MCP and write it with line numbers stripped.\"\"\"\nimport subprocess, json, re, sys\n\nrepo = sys.argv[1]\npath = sys.argv[2]\nout  = sys.argv[3]\n\nresult = subprocess.run(\n    ['curl', '-s', '-X', 'POST', 'http://127.0.0.1:18973/mcp',\n     '-H', 'Content-Type: application/json',\n     '-d', json.dumps({\n         \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\", \"id\": 1, \"method\": \"tools/call\",\n         \"params\": {\"name\": \"read_file\", \"arguments\": {\n             \"repo\": repo, \"path\": path\n         }}\n     })],\n    capture_output=True, text=True\n)\n\nfor line in result.stdout.split('\\n'):\n    if line.startswith('data:'):\n        data = json.loads(line[5:])\n        if 'result' in data:\n            for item in data['result']['content']:\n                if item['type'] == 'text':\n                    inner = json.loads(item['text'])\n                    raw = inner['content']\n                    text = re.sub(r'^\\d+: ', '', raw, flags=re.MULTILINE)\n                    with open(out, 'w') as f:\n                        f.write(text)\n                    print(f\"OK: {len(text.splitlines())} lines -> {out}\")\n                    sys.exit(0)\n\nprint(\"ERROR fetching\", path, file=sys.stderr)\nsys.exit(1)\nEOF\nchmod +x /tmp/fetch_file.py\necho \"Script ready\"",
  "security_risk": "MEDIUM"
}

Output

"cat > /tmp/fetch_file.py << 'EOF'\n#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"Fetch a file from Sourcegraph via local MCP and write it with line numbers stripped.\"\"\"\nimport subprocess, json, re, sys\nrepo = sys.argv[1]\npath = sys.argv[2]\nout  = sys.argv[3]\nresult = subprocess.run(\n    ['curl', '-s', '-X', 'POST', 'http://127.0.0.1:18973/mcp',\n     '-H', 'Content-Type: application/json',\n     '-d', json.dumps({\n         \"jsonrpc\": \"2.0\", \"id\": 1, \"method\": \"tools/call\",\n         \"params\": {\"name\": \"read_file\", \"arguments\": {\n             \"repo\": repo, \"path\": path\n         }}\n     })],\n    capture_output=True, text=True\n)\nfor line in result.stdout.split('\\n'):\n    if line.startswith('data:'):\n        data = json.loads(line[5:])\n        if 'result' in data:\n            for item in data['result']['content']:\n                if item['type'] == 'text':\n                    inner = json.loads(item['text'])\n                    raw = inner['content']\n                    text = re.sub(r'^\\d+: ', '', raw, flags=re.MULTILINE)\n                    with open(out, 'w') as f:\n                        f.write(text)\n                    print(f\"OK: {len(text.splitlines())} lines -> {out}\")\n                    sys.exit(0)\nprint(\"ERROR fetching\", path, file=sys.stderr)\nsys.exit(1)\nEOF\n\n\nchmod +x /tmp/fetch_file.py\n\n\necho \"Script ready\"\nScript ready"
